epigraph.text:
Where two or three gather in my name, there am I 
with them.

content.text:
A **system of linear equations** consists of two or more linear 
equations with the same variables. The **solution** is the set of 
values that satisfies all equations simultaneously.

content.title: System of Linear Equations
content.content:
A **system of linear equations** is a 
set of two or more linear equations with the same variables. A 
**solution** is an ordered pair (or triple, etc.) that satisfies 
all equations in the system.

content.text:
Graphically, each linear equation represents a line. The solution to 
the system is the point where the lines intersect.

content.title: Solving by Graphing
content.content:
1. Graph each equation on the same coordinate plane.

2. Identify the point of intersection (if any).

3. Check the solution in both original equations.

content.title: Solving by graphing
content.problem:
Solve the system by graphing:
\[
 \begin{cases}
 y = 2x - 1 \\
 y = -x + 5
 \end{cases}
\]

content.title: Classification of Linear Systems
content.content:
A system of two linear equations in two variables has exactly one of 
three outcomes:

1. **One solution** (lines intersect at one point): 
 The system is **consistent** and **independent**.

2. **No solution** (lines are parallel): 
 The system is **inconsistent**.

3. **Infinitely many solutions** (lines are identical): 
 The system is **consistent** and **dependent**.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: No solution---parallel lines
Infinitely many solutions---same line
content.solution:
Divide the second equation by 2: $2x + y = 4$.

Both equations represent the same line! Every point on the line is a 
solution.

**Solution:** Infinitely many solutions. The solution set is 
$\{(x, y) : 2x + y = 4\}$, or equivalently, $\{(x, 4 - 2x) : x \in \R\}$.

The system is consistent and dependent.

content.text:
You can classify a system by comparing the equations in slope-intercept 
form:

content.title: Classifying without solving
content.problem:
Classify each system without solving.

(a) $\begin{cases} y = 3x - 2 \\ y = -x + 6 \end{cases}$ 
(b) $\begin{cases} y = \frac{1}{2}x + 4 \\ 2y = x + 8 \end{cases}$ 
(c) $\begin{cases} 3x - y = 5 \\ 6x - 2y = 7 \end{cases}$
content.solution:
(a) Different slopes (3 and $-1$): **One solution**.

(b) Rewrite second equation: $y = \frac{1}{2}x + 4$. Same equation: 
**Infinitely many solutions**.

(c) First: $y = 3x - 5$. Second: $y = 3x - \frac{7}{2}$. Same slope, 
different intercepts: **No solution**.

content.text: While graphing provides visual insight, it has limitations:

items:
**Precision:** If the solution involves fractions (like 
 $(\frac{2}{3}, \frac{5}{7})$), reading exact values from a graph 
 is difficult.

**Scale:** If the solution is far from the origin (like 
 $(150, -200)$), the graph becomes impractical.

content.text:
For these reasons, algebraic methods (substitution and elimination) are 
often more practical. We'll explore those in the next sections.

marginNotes.text:
Do two walk together unless they have agreed 
to do so?

marginNotes.author: Aristotle
marginNotes.text: The whole is greater than the sum of its parts.
